text: Figure 15.9 Progressive growing. a) The generator is initially trained to create very small $4 \times 4$ images, and the discriminator to identify if these images are synthesized or downsampled real images. b) After training at this low-resolution terminates,
15.3 Progressive growing, minibatch discrimination, and truncation

The Wasserstein formulation makes GAN training more stable. However, further machinery is needed to generate high-quality images. We now review progressive growing, minibatch discrimination, and truncation, which all improve output quality.

In progressive growing (figure 15.9), we first train a GAN that synthesizes $4 \times 4$ images using an architecture similar to the DCGAN. Then we add subsequent layers to the generator, which upsample the representation and perform further processing to crea
